course_title: Foundations in Politics, Policy and International Relations

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a well-founded knowledge of the key ideas and issues in the field of politics.
Demonstrate acquired conceptual and analytical capacities, along with an ability to apply these skills to understand concrete social, political and historical processes in politics.
Develop intellectual tools and skills that will make you more competitive in your future professional activities in a variety of fields and positions.

assessments.task_description:
In weeks 1-11, students will spend 10 minutes writing a reflection on that week's content and seminar discussion. Each reflection is worth up to 2.5 marks, and should comprise a broader summary of the week's content (1 mark) and analysis/personal reflection (1.5 marks). Students' best 8 attempts will be recorded in Gradebook (for a maximum of 20 marks). More information about the reflections will be provided in the first class of the semester.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Reflections will be handwritten, without the use of notes, and submitted to the course coordinator at the end of class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
In this task, students will produce an essay plan of no more than 1500 words. The course coordinator will provide feedback on the plan that students will then incorporate into their essay.

The plan should include:

  • Your essay title/topic
  • An annotated bibliography, describing and critiquing at least 4 sources that help you demonstrate the gap or puzzle the essay will address
  • The research question
  • An argument plan, including your core argument and reasons in support of that argument
  • An AI use case, detailing how and why AI will be used in the research and writing process, with reference to UQ policy and best practice

More information about the essay plan assessment will be available on Blackboard, and we will discuss the essay plan in class in more detail.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students should submit their essay plan via the Turnitin link on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will write a 3,000 word essay on one of the topics from the course. It is expected that students will make some small original research contribution. Emphasis should be on assessing relevant literature, identifying a gap in the literature, and how your research fills this gap. Further details and examples will be provided in class throughout the year.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In the final class of the year, students will participate in a poster session to present their essay. During this session, students will be expected to present and discuss their essay with the course coordinator without the use of notes. In addition, students will have the opportunity to mingle and learn about other students’ research.

If you are unable to attend the poster session due to exceptional circumstances / illness (relevant supporting documentation is required), please contact the course coordinator before 8am on the day of the poster session.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: What is politics?

In this introductory seminar we will discuss some of the fundamental building blocks of what makes something political.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: What is politics? In this introductory seminar we will discuss some of the fundamental building blocks of what makes something political.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Realism

The next three seminars will examine some traditions/schools of thought in International Relations. This week we will introduce Realism.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Realism The next three seminars will examine some traditions/schools of thought in International Relations. This week we will introduce Realism.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Liberalism

This week we will examine Liberalism and how it emerged from a critique of how Realist scholars theorize anarchy.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Liberalism This week we will examine Liberalism and how it emerged from a critique of how Realist scholars theorize anarchy.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Constructivism

This seminar will examine Constructivism and how ideas, norms, and social interactions can structure security and economic relations.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4: Constructivism This seminar will examine Constructivism and how ideas, norms, and social interactions can structure security and economic relations.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Postcolonialism

In this seminar we look at influential theories of postcolonial and Indigenous scholars.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Postcolonialism In this seminar we look at influential theories of postcolonial and Indigenous scholars.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: No class

No class. Essay plans due on 4 September.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: No class No class. Essay plans due on 4 September.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Migration

We analyze migration’s economic and political drivers, ethical dilemmas, and governance challenges in a globalized world.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Migration We analyze migration’s economic and political drivers, ethical dilemmas, and governance challenges in a globalized world.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Domestic politics

We begin by examining the seminal text Exit, Voice, and Loyalty and examine if non-violent civil resistance can be effective.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: Domestic politics We begin by examining the seminal text Exit, Voice, and Loyalty and examine if non-violent civil resistance can be effective.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Gender & inequality

This week highlights how gender and economic inequality intersect with political systems and global governance.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9: Gender & inequality This week highlights how gender and economic inequality intersect with political systems and global governance.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: Contemporary conflicts

We analyse recent conflicts, including the war in Ukraine, and their broader geopolitical and security implications.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 10: Contemporary conflicts We analyse recent conflicts, including the war in Ukraine, and their broader geopolitical and security implications.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: Environmental politics

We will examine international climate politics and the distributive conflicts shaping climate policies.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 11: Environmental politics We will examine international climate politics and the distributive conflicts shaping climate policies.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Essay workshop

This week, we will participate in an essay workshop. In the first half, I will provide some essay writing tips and tricks. In the second half, students will present their essays to their peers in small groups, and exchange constructive feedback, critiques, and provocations.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12: Essay workshop This week, we will participate in an essay workshop. In the first half, I will provide some essay writing tips and tricks. In the second half, students will present their essays to their peers in small groups, and exchange constructive feedback, critiques, and provocations.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Poster session

Students will participate in a poster session for their research projects.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 13: Poster session Students will participate in a poster session for their research projects.
